name: Abnormal Psychology
description: An examination of the nature, causes, classification, and treatment of psychological disorders, with emphasis on the DSM-5 framework and current research.

modules.title: Defining Abnormal Behavior
modules.objectives: Define psychological abnormality using the 4 Ds framework, distinguish statistical from functional definitions, and evaluate the role of cultural context.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 15 — for background reading on Defining Abnormal Behavior before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Apply the 4 Ds framework (deviance, dysfunction, distress, danger) to three different behaviors. For each, determine whether it meets the criteria for a psychological disorder and explain which criteria are or are not met. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the Defining Abnormal Behavior module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: History & Models of Psychopathology
modules.titleZh: 精神病理学的历史与模型
modules.objectives: Trace the history of mental illness from ancient times through modern era, compare the medical, psychodynamic, behavioral, cognitive, and sociocultural models.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 15.1 — for background reading on History & Models of Psychopathology before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Compare how the biological, psychodynamic, and cognitive-behavioral models would each explain major depressive disorder. Write a 300-word analysis identifying the strengths and limitations of each model. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the History & Models of Psychopathology module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Classification: DSM-5
modules.objectives: Describe the purpose and structure of the DSM-5, explain the advantages and criticisms of psychiatric classification, and distinguish categorical from dimensional approaches.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 15.1 — for background reading on Classification: DSM-5 before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Research one criticism of the DSM-5 classification system (e.g., overdiagnosis, cultural bias, categorical vs. dimensional debate). Write a 300-word argument for or against the criticism, using specific examples. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the Classification: DSM-5 module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Anxiety Disorders
modules.objectives: Distinguish specific phobia, social anxiety disorder, panic disorder, and generalized anxiety disorder; explain biological and cognitive factors in anxiety; evaluate treatment approaches.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 15.4 — for background reading on Anxiety Disorders before the quiz and assignment

Include the characteristic symptoms, a likely triggering event, and how biological and cognitive factors contribute to the development and maintenance of the disorder. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the Anxiety Disorders module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: OCD & Related Disorders
modules.objectives: Describe the symptoms of OCD, body dysmorphic disorder, and hoarding disorder; explain the role of thought-action fusion; and evaluate exposure and response prevention treatment.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 15.4 — for background reading on OCD & Related Disorders before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.

Using a specific example of an OCD presentation, describe how exposure and response prevention (ERP) breaks the cycle. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the OCD & Related Disorders module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. modules.title: Trauma & PTSD

Create a detailed clinical profile of a fictional combat veteran presenting with PTSD, identifying one symptom from each cluster. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: examples, evidence, observations, concepts, or analysis from the Trauma & PTSD module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. modules.objectives: Describe the diagnostic criteria for major depressive disorder and persistent depressive disorder, compare biological and cognitive explanations, and evaluate pharmacological and psychotherapy treatments.

modules.objectives: Distinguish Bipolar I from Bipolar II disorder and cyclothymia, describe the features of manic episodes, and evaluate mood-stabilizing medications and psychosocial interventions.

Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. modules.title: Psychotic Disorders: Schizophrenia
modules.titleZh: 精神病性障碍：精神分裂症
modules.objectives: Identify positive and negative symptoms of schizophrenia, describe the dopamine hypothesis and structural brain differences, and evaluate antipsychotic medication and psychosocial rehabilitation.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 15.7 — for background reading on Psychotic Disorders: Schizophrenia before the quiz and assignment

For each symptom, provide a specific behavioral example. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. modules.title: Personality Disorders
modules.objectives: Define personality disorders, describe Cluster A, B, and C groupings, explain borderline and antisocial personality disorders in depth, and evaluate the role of early experiences and biology.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 15.9 — for background reading on Personality Disorders before the quiz and assignment

quizQuestions.question: The '4 Ds' framework for defining abnormal behavior includes deviance, distress, dysfunction, and:
quizQuestions.answer: Danger
quizQuestions.explanation: The 4 Ds are deviance (from norms), distress (subjective suffering), dysfunction (impaired functioning), and danger (to self or others).

quizQuestions.question: A behavior that is statistically rare but not necessarily disordered is an example of:
quizQuestions.answer: Statistical deviance without disorder
quizQuestions.explanation: Statistical rarity alone does not define a disorder — genius-level IQ is statistically rare but not disordered. Context and impairment are essential.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? The '4 Ds' framework for defining abnormal behavior includes deviance, distress, dysfunction, and:
quizQuestions.answer: Danger
quizQuestions.explanation: The 4 Ds are deviance (from norms), distress (subjective suffering), dysfunction (impaired functioning), and danger (to self or others).

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Which approach defines abnormality based on deviation from the average in a population?
quizQuestions.answer: Statistical approach
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "Statistical approach" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: The medical model of psychopathology views mental disorders as:
quizQuestions.answer: Diseases with biological causes
quizQuestions.explanation: The medical (biomedical) model conceptualizes mental disorders as illnesses with biological etiologies, treated with medical interventions.

quizQuestions.question: The diathesis-stress model proposes that mental disorders develop when:
options: Biological vulnerability interacts with environmental stress
Social support is lacking in early life
quizQuestions.answer: Biological vulnerability interacts with environmental stress
quizQuestions.explanation: The diathesis-stress model: a predisposition (diathesis) combined with stressful life events leads to the expression of a disorder.

quizQuestions.question: The behavioral model explains psychopathology primarily through:
quizQuestions.answer: Learned associations and reinforcement histories
quizQuestions.explanation: The behavioral model proposes that abnormal behavior is learned through classical or operant conditioning and can be unlearned through behavior therapy.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? The medical model of psychopathology views mental disorders as:
quizQuestions.answer: Diseases with biological causes
quizQuestions.explanation: The medical (biomedical) model conceptualizes mental disorders as illnesses with biological etiologies, treated with medical interventions.

quizQuestions.question: In Classification: DSM-5, The DSM-5 is published by:
quizQuestions.answer: American Psychiatric Association (APA)
quizQuestions.explanation: The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5) is published by the American Psychiatric Association.

quizQuestions.question: A key advantage of categorical classification (DSM-5) is:
options: It captures the full range of symptom severity
It provides clear, standardized criteria facilitating diagnosis and communication
It eliminates cultural bias in diagnosis
It avoids stigma associated with diagnostic labels
quizQuestions.answer: It provides clear, standardized criteria facilitating diagnosis and communication
quizQuestions.explanation: Categorical diagnosis provides clear diagnostic thresholds that enable consistent communication among clinicians and researchers.

quizQuestions.question: A major criticism of the DSM is that diagnostic labels may:
options: Create stigma and influence how others perceive the person
Be too flexible to apply consistently
quizQuestions.answer: Create stigma and influence how others perceive the person
quizQuestions.explanation: Diagnostic labels can stigmatize individuals and create self-fulfilling prophecies, influencing how clinicians and society perceive the labeled person.

quizQuestions.question: A panic attack is characterized by:
options: Sudden intense fear with physical symptoms peaking within minutes
Fear of a specific identifiable object or situation only
Gradual onset of anxiety over weeks
quizQuestions.answer: Sudden intense fear with physical symptoms peaking within minutes
quizQuestions.explanation: Panic attacks are abrupt surges of intense fear with symptoms (heart racing, chest pain, dizziness) peaking within 10 minutes.

quizQuestions.question: Social anxiety disorder involves intense fear of:
options: Social situations where one may be embarrassed or scrutinized
Specific objects such as spiders or heights
Having a panic attack in public
quizQuestions.answer: Social situations where one may be embarrassed or scrutinized
quizQuestions.explanation: Social anxiety disorder is characterized by intense, persistent fear of social situations in which one may be judged, embarrassed, or humiliated.

quizQuestions.question: Cognitive factors in anxiety disorders typically involve:
options: Overestimation of threat and underestimation of coping ability
quizQuestions.answer: Overestimation of threat and underestimation of coping ability
quizQuestions.explanation: Cognitive models of anxiety emphasize distorted threat appraisals — overestimating the probability and severity of harm and underestimating one's capacity to cope.

quizQuestions.question: The biological basis of anxiety disorders includes dysregulation of:
quizQuestions.answer: GABA and serotonin systems
quizQuestions.explanation: Anxiety disorders are associated with reduced GABAergic (inhibitory) and serotonergic activity, contributing to hyperexcitability and heightened fear responses.

quizQuestions.question: In OCD & Related Disorders, Obsessions in OCD are:
options: Repetitive behaviors performed to reduce anxiety
Unwanted, intrusive thoughts that cause distress
quizQuestions.answer: Unwanted, intrusive thoughts that cause distress
quizQuestions.explanation: Obsessions are recurrent, persistent thoughts, urges, or images that are experienced as intrusive and cause marked anxiety or distress.

quizQuestions.question: In OCD & Related Disorders, Compulsions in OCD are:
options: Repetitive behaviors or mental acts performed to reduce obsession-related distress
quizQuestions.answer: Repetitive behaviors or mental acts performed to reduce obsession-related distress
quizQuestions.explanation: Compulsions are repetitive behaviors (e.g., hand washing, checking) or mental acts (e.g., counting) performed to neutralize obsession-related anxiety.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Obsessions in OCD are:
options: Repetitive behaviors performed to reduce anxiety
Unwanted, intrusive thoughts that cause distress
quizQuestions.answer: Unwanted, intrusive thoughts that cause distress
quizQuestions.explanation: Obsessions are recurrent, persistent thoughts, urges, or images that are experienced as intrusive and cause marked anxiety or distress.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Compulsions in OCD are:
options: Repetitive behaviors or mental acts performed to reduce obsession-related distress
quizQuestions.answer: Repetitive behaviors or mental acts performed to reduce obsession-related distress
quizQuestions.explanation: Compulsions are repetitive behaviors (e.g., hand washing, checking) or mental acts (e.g., counting) performed to neutralize obsession-related anxiety.

quizQuestions.question: DSM-5 requires PTSD symptoms to be present for at least:
quizQuestions.answer: 1 month
quizQuestions.explanation: DSM-5 requires PTSD symptoms to persist for at least one month following the traumatic event for diagnosis.

quizQuestions.question: Intrusion symptoms of PTSD include:
quizQuestions.answer: Flashbacks, nightmares, and intrusive memories
quizQuestions.explanation: Intrusion (re-experiencing) symptoms involve involuntary return to the traumatic event through flashbacks, nightmares, and distressing memories.

quizQuestions.question: EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing) is used primarily to treat:
quizQuestions.answer: PTSD and trauma-related disorders
quizQuestions.explanation: EMDR is an evidence-based therapy for PTSD that uses bilateral stimulation (e.g., guided eye movements) during recall of traumatic memories to facilitate processing.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? DSM-5 requires PTSD symptoms to be present for at least:
quizQuestions.answer: 1 month
quizQuestions.explanation: DSM-5 requires PTSD symptoms to persist for at least one month following the traumatic event for diagnosis.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Which brain structure is most implicated in the exaggerated fear responses seen in ptsd?
quizQuestions.answer: Amygdala
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "Amygdala" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: Beck's cognitive triad in depression consists of negative views about:
quizQuestions.answer: Self, world, and future
quizQuestions.explanation: Beck's cognitive triad identifies three areas of negative automatic thoughts: oneself (worthless), the world (overwhelming), and the future (hopeless).

quizQuestions.question: DSM-5 criteria for major depressive disorder require at least _____ depressive symptoms for _____ weeks.
quizQuestions.explanation: MDD requires 5+ symptoms including depressed mood or anhedonia for at least 2 weeks to be diagnosed.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Beck's cognitive triad in depression consists of negative views about:
quizQuestions.answer: Self, world, and future
quizQuestions.explanation: Beck's cognitive triad identifies three areas of negative automatic thoughts: oneself (worthless), the world (overwhelming), and the future (hopeless).

quizQuestions.question: Bipolar I disorder is distinguished from Bipolar II by:
options: At least one full manic episode
At least one hypomanic episode without full mania
quizQuestions.answer: At least one full manic episode
quizQuestions.explanation: Bipolar I requires at least one full manic episode (lasting at least 7 days), which may or may not be accompanied by depressive episodes.

quizQuestions.question: A manic episode is characterized by:
options: Abnormally elevated or expansive mood, increased energy, and decreased need for sleep
Severe cognitive impairment and disorganized speech
quizQuestions.answer: Abnormally elevated or expansive mood, increased energy, and decreased need for sleep
quizQuestions.explanation: DSM-5 defines a manic episode as a distinct period of abnormally elevated, expansive, or irritable mood and increased goal-directed activity or energy.

quizQuestions.question: Lithium is most commonly used as a mood stabilizer to treat:
quizQuestions.answer: Bipolar disorder
quizQuestions.explanation: Lithium is a first-line mood stabilizer that reduces the frequency and severity of both manic and depressive episodes in bipolar disorder.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Bipolar I disorder is distinguished from Bipolar II by:
options: At least one full manic episode
At least one hypomanic episode without full mania
quizQuestions.answer: At least one full manic episode
quizQuestions.explanation: Bipolar I requires at least one full manic episode (lasting at least 7 days), which may or may not be accompanied by depressive episodes.

quizQuestions.question: Positive symptoms of schizophrenia are best described as:
options: Absences or deficits in normal behavior
Excesses or additions beyond normal behavior
Symptoms present only during acute episodes
quizQuestions.answer: Excesses or additions beyond normal behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Positive symptoms involve additions to normal behavior (hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech/behavior) as opposed to the deficits characteristic of negative symptoms.

quizQuestions.question: Flat affect, alogia, and avolition are examples of _____ symptoms of schizophrenia.
quizQuestions.answer: Negative
quizQuestions.explanation: Negative symptoms are deficits in normal functioning: flat affect (reduced emotional expression), alogia (reduced speech), and avolition (lack of motivation).

quizQuestions.question: Which mechanism does the dopamine hypothesis connect most closely with positive symptoms of schizophrenia?
options: Deficient dopamine activity in the prefrontal cortex
Excess dopamine activity in mesolimbic pathways
quizQuestions.answer: Excess dopamine activity in mesolimbic pathways
quizQuestions.explanation: The dopamine hypothesis proposes that excess dopamine transmission in mesolimbic pathways produces positive symptoms of schizophrenia.

quizQuestions.question: Personality disorders are distinguished from Axis I disorders by being:
options: Caused by identifiable biological factors only
Pervasive, inflexible, and long-standing patterns beginning in early adulthood
quizQuestions.answer: Pervasive, inflexible, and long-standing patterns beginning in early adulthood
quizQuestions.explanation: Personality disorders are enduring, maladaptive patterns of inner experience and behavior that are pervasive and stable across situations, starting in early adulthood.

quizQuestions.question: Cluster B personality disorders are characterized by:
quizQuestions.answer: Dramatic, emotional, erratic behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Cluster B includes borderline, antisocial, histrionic, and narcissistic personality disorders — all involving dramatic, emotional, or erratic patterns.

quizQuestions.question: Antisocial personality disorder requires a history of conduct disorder before age:
quizQuestions.explanation: DSM-5 requires evidence of conduct disorder with onset before age 15 for an antisocial personality disorder diagnosis, plus current behavior since age 18.

quizQuestions.question: Borderline personality disorder (BPD) is characterized by:
options: Instability in relationships, self-image, emotions, and impulsivity
quizQuestions.answer: Instability in relationships, self-image, emotions, and impulsivity
quizQuestions.explanation: BPD involves unstable relationships, identity disturbances, emotional dysregulation, and impulsive behaviors, often with intense fear of abandonment.

quizQuestions.question: Anorexia nervosa is characterized by:
options: Restriction of food intake leading to significantly low body weight, body image distortion, and intense fear of gaining weight
Recurrent episodes of binge eating without compensatory behaviors
Avoidance of specific foods due to fear of choking
quizQuestions.answer: Restriction of food intake leading to significantly low body weight, body image distortion, and intense fear of gaining weight
quizQuestions.explanation: Anorexia nervosa involves restriction of energy intake, intense fear of weight gain, and disturbed body image resulting in dangerously low body weight.

quizQuestions.question: The key feature distinguishing bulimia nervosa from anorexia is:
options: Binge eating followed by compensatory behavior, with body weight typically at or above normal
quizQuestions.answer: Binge eating followed by compensatory behavior, with body weight typically at or above normal
quizQuestions.explanation: Bulimia involves recurrent binge eating followed by purging (vomiting, laxatives) or non-purging (fasting, exercise) behaviors, usually at normal or above-normal weight.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Anorexia nervosa is characterized by:
options: Restriction of food intake leading to significantly low body weight, body image distortion, and intense fear of gaining weight
Recurrent episodes of binge eating without compensatory behaviors
Avoidance of specific foods due to fear of choking
quizQuestions.answer: Restriction of food intake leading to significantly low body weight, body image distortion, and intense fear of gaining weight
quizQuestions.explanation: Anorexia nervosa involves restriction of energy intake, intense fear of weight gain, and disturbed body image resulting in dangerously low body weight.

quizQuestions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? The key feature distinguishing bulimia nervosa from anorexia is:
options: Binge eating followed by compensatory behavior, with body weight typically at or above normal
quizQuestions.answer: Binge eating followed by compensatory behavior, with body weight typically at or above normal
quizQuestions.explanation: Bulimia involves recurrent binge eating followed by purging (vomiting, laxatives) or non-purging (fasting, exercise) behaviors, usually at normal or above-normal weight.

questions.question: The 4 Ds framework defines abnormal behavior using which four criteria?
questions.answer: Dysfunction, Distress, Deviance, Danger
questions.explanation: The 4 Ds are a framework for identifying psychological disorders: deviance from norms, distress (subjective suffering), dysfunction (impaired functioning), and danger.

questions.question: The diathesis-stress model proposes mental disorders develop from:
options: An interaction between biological vulnerability and environmental stress
questions.answer: An interaction between biological vulnerability and environmental stress
questions.explanation: The diathesis-stress model: a biological predisposition is activated by environmental stressors to produce disorder.

questions.question: The DSM-5 is primarily used to:
questions.answer: Classify and diagnose psychological disorders
questions.explanation: The DSM-5 provides standardized diagnostic criteria for classifying and diagnosing mental disorders.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? A panic attack is best characterized by:
options: Sudden intense fear peaking within minutes with physical symptoms
A long-term depressed mood lasting at least two weeks
A persistent pattern of grandiosity across settings
A gradual loss of memory over several years
questions.answer: Sudden intense fear peaking within minutes with physical symptoms
questions.explanation: A panic attack is a sudden surge of intense fear or discomfort that reaches a peak quickly and includes physical or cognitive symptoms.

questions.question: The OCD cycle is: intrusive thought → anxiety → compulsion → temporary relief → which outcome?
options: Development of insight about the irrationality of the thought
questions.answer: Reinforcement of the compulsion
questions.explanation: Temporary relief from compulsions negatively reinforces the behavior, strengthening the cycle and maintaining OCD.

questions.question: PTSD requires symptoms to persist for at least:
questions.answer: 1 month
questions.explanation: DSM-5 requires at least one month of PTSD symptoms for diagnosis; earlier presentations may be classified as Acute Stress Disorder.

questions.question: Beck's cognitive triad identifies negative views of:
questions.answer: Self, world, and future
questions.explanation: Beck's cognitive triad describes three areas of negative automatic thinking: the self, the world, and the future.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? A key feature of anorexia nervosa is:
options: Restriction leading to significantly low body weight with intense fear of weight gain or body-image disturbance
Binge eating followed by compensatory behaviors without significantly low body weight
Repeated panic attacks focused on social judgment
Loss of contact with reality for at least six months
questions.answer: Restriction leading to significantly low body weight with intense fear of weight gain or body-image disturbance
questions.explanation: Anorexia nervosa centers on significantly low body weight along with fear of gaining weight and disturbance in body image or self-evaluation.

questions.question: Rosenhan's pseudopatient study revealed that:
options: Schizophrenia is easily detected by clinicians
Psychiatric diagnosis is highly reliable and objective
Diagnostic labels bias how clinicians interpret subsequent behavior
Patients in psychiatric hospitals recover faster than outpatients
questions.answer: Diagnostic labels bias how clinicians interpret subsequent behavior
questions.explanation: Once labeled 'schizophrenic,' the pseudopatients' normal behavior was interpreted through that diagnostic lens, demonstrating labeling effects.

questions.question: Social anxiety disorder is defined by intense fear of:
options: Social situations where embarrassment or scrutiny is possible
Specific objects like spiders or heights
Having a panic attack in any setting
questions.answer: Social situations where embarrassment or scrutiny is possible
questions.explanation: Social anxiety disorder involves fear and avoidance of social situations in which one fears negative evaluation, embarrassment, or humiliation.

questions.question: Thought-action fusion in OCD is the belief that:
options: Compulsions prevent intrusive thoughts from recurring
Thinking something bad is morally equivalent to doing it
questions.answer: Thinking something bad is morally equivalent to doing it
questions.explanation: Thought-action fusion is the cognitive distortion that having a bad thought is as morally wrong as acting on it, or that it can make the event happen.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? The amygdala is most implicated in the exaggerated fear responses of:
questions.answer: PTSD and anxiety disorders
questions.explanation: The amygdala is strongly involved in fear learning and threat detection, which are important concepts in PTSD and anxiety research.

questions.question: Generalized Anxiety Disorder involves worry that is:
options: Excessive, uncontrollable, and spans multiple life domains
questions.answer: Excessive, uncontrollable, and spans multiple life domains
questions.explanation: GAD involves persistent, uncontrollable worry about many different concerns (e.g., health, finances, relationships) lasting at least 6 months.

questions.question: The medical model views mental disorders as:
options: Diseases with biological causes requiring medical treatment
Consequences of social and cultural stressors
questions.answer: Diseases with biological causes requiring medical treatment
questions.explanation: The biomedical model conceptualizes mental disorders as illnesses analogous to physical diseases, with neurobiological etiologies and medical treatments.

questions.question: ERP (Exposure and Response Prevention) treats OCD by:
options: Exposing the client to the feared stimulus while preventing compulsive response
Using medication to eliminate intrusive thoughts
questions.answer: Exposing the client to the feared stimulus while preventing compulsive response
questions.explanation: ERP prevents the compulsive neutralizing behavior while exposing the client to the feared cue, allowing habituation and breaking the OCD reinforcement cycle.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Bipolar I disorder requires at least one full manic episode lasting:
options: At least one week, or any duration if hospitalization is required
Only a few minutes during a panic attack
questions.answer: At least one week, or any duration if hospitalization is required
questions.explanation: A manic episode generally lasts at least one week unless hospitalization is necessary sooner.

questions.question: Positive symptoms of schizophrenia include hallucinations, delusions, and:
questions.answer: Disorganized speech
questions.explanation: Positive symptoms add to normal experience: hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized speech (and behavior) are all positive symptoms.

questions.question: The dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia proposes that positive symptoms result from:
questions.answer: Excess mesolimbic dopamine activity
questions.explanation: Excess dopamine in mesolimbic pathways (especially nucleus accumbens) is linked to positive symptoms; antipsychotics work by blocking D2 receptors.

questions.question: Borderline personality disorder is characterized by:
options: Dramatic emotional instability, unstable relationships, and fear of abandonment
Social withdrawal and restricted emotional expression
questions.answer: Dramatic emotional instability, unstable relationships, and fear of abandonment
questions.explanation: BPD involves marked instability in relationships, self-image, emotions, and significant impulsivity, often driven by intense fear of abandonment.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) was developed specifically for:
questions.answer: Borderline personality disorder
questions.explanation: DBT was originally developed for borderline personality disorder and emotion-regulation difficulties.

questions.question: The mesolimbic dopamine pathway is central to drug addiction because it mediates:
options: The rewarding and reinforcing effects of drugs
questions.answer: The rewarding and reinforcing effects of drugs
questions.explanation: Drugs activate the mesolimbic dopamine system (ventral tegmental area to nucleus accumbens), generating intense reward signals that drive compulsive drug-seeking.

questions.question: ADHD is characterized by inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity that:
options: Is present in at least two settings and causes functional impairment
Is diagnosed exclusively by behavioral observation
questions.answer: Is present in at least two settings and causes functional impairment
questions.explanation: DSM-5 requires ADHD symptoms to be present in at least two settings (e.g., home and school) and cause significant functional impairment.

questions.question: Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) for ASD uses _____ to teach skills and reduce challenging behaviors.
questions.answer: Reinforcement-based behavioral principles
questions.explanation: ABA systematically uses positive reinforcement and other behavioral techniques to teach functional skills and reduce maladaptive behaviors in individuals with ASD.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? A major sociocultural factor contributing to eating disorders is:
options: Pressure around thin ideals, body image, and weight-focused social standards
questions.answer: Pressure around thin ideals, body image, and weight-focused social standards
questions.explanation: Sociocultural explanations focus on body ideals, media messages, peer norms, and weight-focused expectations rather than a single biological factor.

questions.question: Naltrexone treats opioid and alcohol use disorders by:
options: Producing cross-tolerance to the addictive substance
Blocking opioid receptors and reducing the rewarding effects of the substance
Increasing dopamine in the reward pathway
questions.answer: Blocking opioid receptors and reducing the rewarding effects of the substance
questions.explanation: Naltrexone is an opioid antagonist that blocks the euphoric and reinforcing effects of opioids and reduces craving in alcohol use disorder.

questions.question: Antisocial personality disorder (ASPD) requires evidence of conduct disorder before age:
questions.explanation: DSM-5 requires a history of conduct disorder with onset before age 15 as part of the ASPD diagnosis.

questions.question: ECT is most effective for treating:
questions.answer: Treatment-resistant severe depression
questions.explanation: ECT produces rapid improvement in severe, treatment-resistant depression, though its mechanism remains incompletely understood.

questions.question: Which answer best completes this statement? The therapeutic alliance is one of the strongest predictors of therapy outcome because:
options: A trusting relationship supports engagement, honesty, and use of therapeutic techniques
It guarantees symptom remission without treatment work
It replaces the need for assessment
It works only when clients and counselors share identical backgrounds
questions.answer: A trusting relationship supports engagement, honesty, and use of therapeutic techniques
questions.explanation: A strong therapeutic alliance helps clients engage with treatment tasks and communicate openly; it is not a guarantee of outcome.

questions.question: Autism Spectrum Disorder is defined by deficits in social communication and:
options: Restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior and interests
questions.answer: Restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior and interests
questions.explanation: DSM-5 defines ASD by two core feature domains: social communication deficits and restricted, repetitive behaviors/interests.

questions.question: Binge-eating disorder (BED) differs from bulimia nervosa in that BED does not involve:
options: Episodes of eating large amounts of food
questions.answer: Regular compensatory behaviors (purging, excessive exercise)
questions.explanation: BED involves recurrent uncontrolled binge eating but, unlike bulimia, does not include regular use of compensatory behaviors to prevent weight gain.

questions.question: The cognitive model explains psychopathology primarily through:
options: Maladaptive thought patterns and cognitive distortions
questions.answer: Maladaptive thought patterns and cognitive distortions
questions.explanation: The cognitive model (Beck) proposes that distorted thinking creates and maintains emotional disorders.

questions.question: Which term correctly completes this statement? Flat affect in schizophrenia is classified as a _____ symptom
questions.answer: Negative
questions.explanation: Flat affect is a reduction in emotional expression, so it is classified as a negative symptom.

questions.question: Psychoeducation for bipolar disorder helps primarily by:
options: Teaching patients to restructure cognitive distortions
Improving understanding of the disorder and treatment, enhancing adherence
Providing social support through group therapy
questions.answer: Improving understanding of the disorder and treatment, enhancing adherence
questions.explanation: Psychoeducation helps patients and families understand bipolar disorder's course, recognize warning signs, and adhere to medication — all of which reduce relapse rates.

questions.question: Learned helplessness as a model of depression proposes that:
options: Depression is caused by excessive dopamine activity
Exposure to uncontrollable stressors leads to passive resignation and depression
Cognitive distortions cause negative mood directly
questions.answer: Exposure to uncontrollable stressors leads to passive resignation and depression
questions.explanation: Seligman's learned helplessness model: repeated uncontrollable negative events produce the belief that outcomes are uncontrollable, leading to motivational, cognitive, and emotional deficits resembling depression.

questions.question: A major barrier to mental healthcare access in the United States is:
options: Excessive number of mental health professionals
Insurance companies providing unlimited mental health coverage
Stigma associated with seeking mental health treatment
Overdiagnosis reducing the demand for treatment
questions.answer: Stigma associated with seeking mental health treatment
questions.explanation: Mental health stigma discourages many individuals from seeking help; combined with cost, insurance gaps, and provider shortages, it constitutes a major access barrier.
